does: This paper introduces EvoPref, a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for LLM alignment that maintains populations of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters optimized simultaneously across helpfulness, harmlessness, and honesty objectives using NSGA-II selection with archive-based diversity preservation. The core contribution is demonstrating that population-based evolutionary methods discover substantially more diverse model alignments than gradient-based preference optimization. On standard benchmarks, EvoPref achieves 18% improvement in preference coverage (median 82.5% versus 70.0% for ORPO) and reduces preference collapse rates by 47% (11.0% versus 20.6%), while maintaining competitive alignment quality at 75.5% on RewardBench compared to 75.0% for baseline ORPO.
connects: This work builds on two main research threads: gradient-based preference optimization methods like ORPO and DPO that align LLMs by optimizing over preference pairs, and multi-objective evolutionary algorithms including NSGA-II that balance multiple competing objectives. The paper directly responds to the known problem of preference collapse in gradient descent-based alignment, where models converge to narrow behavioral modes despite diverse human preferences. It extends recent theoretical analysis of MOEA runtime complexity and applies population-based evolutionary strategies to the LLM alignment problem, connecting evolutionary optimization traditions to modern language model fine-tuning via LoRA adapters.
enables: This work opens several research directions for evolutionary approaches to AI safety and alignment. By demonstrating that population-based methods can preserve preference diversity beyond gradient descent, it suggests evolutionary algorithms as a general framework for multi-objective alignment problems. The approach could be extended to higher-dimensional preference spaces with more than three objectives, scaled to larger model architectures beyond LoRA, or combined with hybrid methods that leverage gradient information within population updates. The theoretical grounding in MOEA analysis may enable principled design of alignment algorithms that provably maintain diversity, contributing to more robust and value-aligned AI systems that reflect heterogeneous human preferences.
